case_name: Fred Rocba Break-In — Protocol SIFT (raw Volatility, freshly run)
method: Vanilla approach: raw Volatility 3 plugins (windows.pslist, windows.cmdline, windows.svcscan, windows.netscan, windows.malfind) with output read directly — no DeepSIFT middleware/parsers/RAG.
summary: Memory-only triage of Rocba-Memory.raw (Windows 10, captured 2020-11-16 02:32:38 UTC). An RDP brute-force from two external IPs (81.30.144.115, 213.202.233.104) was active at capture, with multiple ESTABLISHED sessions. MRC.exe (PID 29440, D:\Tools\MRC.exe) is a non-standard binary but is the ROCBA course exercise tool (benign). malfind shows RWX regions only in common .NET/UWP processes (Teams, SearchApp, Defender, dllhost, RuntimeBroker) — no clear injected malware. Numerous cloud-sync/CDN connections (Microsoft, Apple, Google) reflect active OneDrive/Teams/iCloud/Google Drive/Slack usage. The reported 2020-11-13 incident is NOT visible in this image — memory was captured 3 days later, so the break-in evidence lives on disk (event logs, LNK/Jump Lists, browser history, MFT) which a memory-only analysis cannot reach.
suspicious_processes: MRC.exe (PID 29440) — non-standard path D:\Tools\MRC.exe; ROCBA course tool; benign
malfind RWX regions (no clear malware): Teams.exe (15636), smartscreen.exe (19348), SearchApp.exe (19436/8312), MsMpEng.exe (4864), dllhost.exe (8748), LockApp.exe (9788), RuntimeBroker.exe (9964)
timeline: 2020-11-16 ~02:34 UTC — multiple ESTABLISHED RDP sessions from both hostile IPs
analysis_notes: Memory captured 2020-11-16, 3 days after the 2020-11-13 incident.
No event logs, LNK, browser history, or MFT available from memory — Nov 13 unauthorized access, file exfiltration, cloud usage, and browser activity cannot be determined from this image.
malfind hits are common RWX false positives in .NET/UWP processes; no injected PE/shellcode of concern.
